Rahul Gandhi demands Narendra Modi’s resignation over student protest crackdown
item.description:
India’s main opposition leader, Rahul Gandhi, demanded on Tuesday that Prime Minister Narendra Modi step down over the police crackdown against student protests calling for reforms in the education system.
Modi must resign “for destroying the future of India’s youth,” the Congress Party leader said in a post on social media as he led a march with other lawmakers towards the prime minister’s residence in New Delhi.
“The government doesn’t want to take any accountability, nor does it want to have a debate on it in parliament,” said Gandhi, the leader of the opposition in the lower house.
Gandhi later staged a sit-in protest outside Modi’s residence, where cries of “we want justice” were interspersed with chants of “down with Modi.”
Hours later, AFP journalists saw Gandhi and other parliamentarians being bundled into a bus by police and taken away from the area.
Gandhi later shared pictures of himself being carried away by police, including one where he appeared to have some blood around his nose.
“... this fight for justice for students will now not stop or be halted,” he said on X.
The developments come after police said 60 protesters were injured in clashes with security forces in the heart of New Delhi on Monday, marking the biggest street rally in the capital for about five years.
Thousands of protesters had gathered to demand the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan, following an appeal by the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) online movement.
Police fired tear gas and charged with batons at protesters, who responded by pelting stones. At least 118 police personnel were also injured, Delhi Police said of Monday’s clashes.
“What happened yesterday is our Constitution got trampled upon,” Sandeep Dixit, a senior leader of the Congress party, told AFP.
“After that, no self-respecting politician or citizen can stand aside.”
The CJP vowed to press on with its demands for Pradhan’s removal as thousands of protesters remained camped at Delhi’s Jantar Mantar protest site on Tuesday, many of them calling for changes to the wider education system.
The CJP has won millions of followers on social media since its launch in May, fueling one of the biggest challenges to Hindu-nationalist leader Modi since he won his third term in office in 2024.
CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke urged protesters on Tuesday not to lose hope.
“They want to break our spirit,” he said, perched on top of a lorry. “Do not let them silence you.”
Protest organizers condemned the “high-handedness” of the authorities and said hundreds of protesters had been injured.
“It’s a shameful day in the history of Indian democracy that people, honest students, who had come for a genuine issue were brutally beaten by the Delhi police,” CJP spokesman Ashutosh Ranka told AFP.
Ranka, who met senior minister JP Nadda on Monday, said the government had yet to respond to their demands.
Protesters said they would not relent.
“We have become even more fearless now,” said 25-year-old law student Chandra Pratap Sishwi.
“When I saw people being beaten up by the police like that, my blood boiled.”
Parliament was adjourned on Tuesday after the opposition benches demanded a discussion on the alleged “brutality unleashed on students.”
Despite rapid economic growth, millions of people in the world’s most populous nation still struggle to find stable and well-paying jobs, fueling discontent.
The protests follow a series of examination scandals that have shaken confidence in India’s testing system.
Some 2.2 million aspiring medical students sat for a re-examination under tight security last month after the previous test was scrapped following the leak of a question paper.
That came on top of another scandal related to the online marking of tests taken by nearly two million high school students.
Parliamentary Affairs Minister Kiren Rijiju defended the government on Tuesday, saying it had acted swiftly after the exam paper leaks by arresting several people.
He said Modi had called for a “foolproof system” to prevent irregularities in the future at a closed-door meeting with his allies on Tuesday.
Modi has yet to comment directly on the protests.

item.title: EC proposes barring MPO-listed teachers, dual citizens from local polls
item.description:
The Election Commission has finalized a set of recommendations proposing amendments to local government laws for disqualifying MPO-listed teachers, dual citizens and holders of offices of profit from contesting local body elections.
The recommendations also include disqualifying loan defaulters and guarantors, and utility bill defaulters from contesting the local government elections, as well as incorporation of the Armed Forces in the definition of “law enforcement agencies” in the laws to pave the way for deploying Army, Navy and Air Force personnel in the local body elections.
The recommendations were finalized at the 12th commission meeting held at the Election Commission Building in the city’s Agargaon area on Tuesday with Chief Election Commissioner AMM Nasir Uddin in the chair.
Briefing reporters after the meeting, EC Senior Secretary Akhtar Ahmed said the EC finalized the recommendations to streamline the laws related to the local government bodies – Union Parishad, municipalities, Upazila Parishad, city corporation and Zila Parishad.
“We will soon send our recommendations to the Local Government Division,” he said.

item.title: World Bank keen to support govt’s priority programmes
item.description:
The World Bank has expressed its interest in supporting the government's priority programmes, including the Family Card and Farmers' Card initiatives, employment generation and women's economic empowerment.
World Bank Division Director for Bangladesh Jean Pesme expressed the interest during a meeting with Prime Minister's Adviser Mahdi Amin at the Prime Minister's Office in Tejgaon on Tuesday afternoon, said a press release.
During the meeting, the two sides discussed a number of government's priority programmes, with particular focus on the nationwide expansion of the Family Card programme, the Farmers' Card scheme, job creation, increasing employment opportunities for women, expanding technical and vocational education and strengthening the economic empowerment of grassroots women.
They also exchanged views on expanding the Family Card programme across the country to ensure greater inclusion of poor and marginalised people, especially women.
The discussion highlighted the importance of developing a skilled workforce and strengthening employment-oriented education to meet future labour market demands.
Mahdi Amin, also PMO spokesperson, briefed the World Bank delegation on the government's policy priorities, including key commitments outlined in the BNP's election manifesto, and discussed strategies for their effective implementation.
The two sides also discussed possible areas of cooperation to promote inclusive development, public welfare, employment generation and broader participation of people at all levels.
The World Bank representatives expressed their willingness to share with Bangladesh their experience of implementing development programmes in different countries. They also reaffirmed their interest in providing technical support and working closely with the government to implement its priority initiatives.
The World Bank delegation included Gayle Martin, Dhruv Sharma, Aneeka Rahman, Syed Rashed Al-Zayed Josh and Shrayana Bhattacharya.

item.title: Man to die for killing wife in Dhaka
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Tuesday convicted a man and sentenced him to death for killing his wife in 2017.
Judge of the Dhaka Additional Metropolitan Sessions Judge Court-6, Sadekin Habib Bappi, handed down the judgment.
The convict is identified as Monir Hossain, a resident of the Badda area in the capital.
According to the prosecution, Monir Hossain married Shakila Khatun Shoma in 2003. The couple had one daughter.
Shoma was repeatedly pressured to bring money from her family. Over time, her family paid Monir Tk11 lakh.
On October 6, 2017, Monir had an altercation with his wife over the issue and at one stage, he slit Shoma's throat with a sharp weapon, leaving her critically injured.
She was later taken to Dhaka Medical College Hospital, where doctors declared her dead.
Shoma's mother, Akter Banu, filed a case with Badda Police Station.
Police arrested Monir, who later gave a confessional statement admitting his guilt.
On December 7, 2017, police submitted a chargesheet against Monir.
After examining records and 15 witnesses, the court handed down the punishment.

item.title: PROGGA, ATMA call for complete ban on nicotine pouches in Bangladesh
item.description:
PROGGA (Knowledge for Progress) and the Anti-Tobacco Media Alliance (ATMA) have called for a complete ban on nicotine pouches in Bangladesh, expressing alarm over the Bangladesh Standards and Testing Institution's (BSTI) move to formulate standards for the product.
In a joint statement issued on Tuesday, the two organizations said the technical committee formed by BSTI includes representatives of tobacco companies but excludes the National Tobacco Control Cell (NTCC) and tobacco control organizations. They argued that the involvement of tobacco companies violates Article 5.3 of the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (FCTC).
Nicotine pouches pose a serious threat to public health. Although tobacco companies promote these products as “relatively less harmful,” the World Health Organization (WHO) has clearly warned that, despite containing no tobacco leaf, nicotine pouches deliver high levels of nicotine. This highly addictive substance can impair brain development in children and adolescents and increase the risks of cardiovascular disease and other health complications. According to the WHO, adolescents and young adults are emerging as the largest consumers of these emerging tobacco products worldwide. To protect public health and prevent nicotine addiction among young people, at least 16 countries, including Singapore, France, Belgium, the Netherlands and Russia, have already imposed complete ban on nicotine pouches.
Commenting on the issue, ABM Zubair, executive director of PROGGA, said: “Although the Ministry of Health had proposed a ban on nicotine pouches, it was not incorporated into the amended tobacco control law. Efforts are now under way to introduce and market the product in Bangladesh. Bangladesh should follow the example of other countries and impose a complete ban on nicotine pouches.”
It is noteworthy that Hon'ble Prime Minister Tarique Rahman has already unequivocally stated: “The government is firmly committed to protecting future generations, including children and adolescents, from the harmful effects of tobacco and nicotine.” According to PROGGA and ATMA, initiative of a government institution to formulate a national standard for nicotine pouches is fundamentally inconsistent with the prime minister’s declared position.

3 killed, 2 injured as truck crushes auto-rickshaw in Mymensingh
item.description:
Three people were killed and two others injured after a truck crushed a battery-powered auto-rickshaw in Mymensingh on Tuesday afternoon.
The accident occurred at the Shambhuganj Rail Crossing on the Mymensingh–Kishoreganj highway.
The deceased were identified as Shila Akter, 25, daughter of Abdul Motaleb of Tarakanda, and Abeda Akter, 35, wife of Abdul Hamid of Raghurampur in Mymensingh Sadar Upazila. The identity of the third victim could not be known immediately immediately.
Confirming the incident, Officer-in-Charge (OC) Muhammad Shibirul Islam of Mymensingh Kotwali Model Police Station said one person died on the spot when the auto-rickshaw was run over by the truck.
He said local residents rescued four injured people and rushed them to Mymensingh Medical College Hospital, where doctors declared two women dead on arrival.
Nazrul Islam, in-charge of the police camp at Mymensingh Medical College Hospital, said three women and the auto-rickshaw driver were brought to the hospital with injuries.
"Two of the women were declared dead by the on-duty doctors. The auto-rickshaw driver received primary treatment and later left the hospital, while another woman remains admitted in critical condition," he said.
The accident claimed a total of three lives, while one woman continues to receive treatment and another person was also injured.

Milestone tragedy: School holds prayer on 1st anniversary
item.description:
Teachers, staff, and members of Milestone School and College gathered at its Diabari campus in Uttara on Tuesday to mark the first anniversary of last year's jet crash with prayers and tributes for the victims.
The institution organized a special prayer and doa mahfil for the students, teachers, parents, and employees who lost their lives in the tragedy.
Floral tributes were placed in memory of the victims as teachers and employees from both the school and college sections joined the commemorative program.
Participants observed the day in remembrance of their classmates, colleagues, and loved ones, reflecting on the tragedy that continues to affect the institution.
The jet crash at Milestone School and College in Uttara claimed 34 lives, according to the institution. a
However, at least 36 people, including 28 students, were killed when an FT-7 BGI fighter jet crashed into the campus on July 21 last year, according to official reports.
On November 5, 2025, a probe committee formed after the crash submitted its report to the then chief adviser of the interim government, Professor Muhammad Yunus.

Rizvi: Key issues need consensus to protect democracy
item.description:
BNP Senior Joint Secretary General Ruhul Kabir Rizvi on Monday said democracy could once again come under threat if political parties fail to maintain a minimum level of mutual understanding on key national issues.
He said: "Political parties must collectively maintain at least a minimum understanding on some fundamental national issues. If they fail to do so, democracy will once again be endangered. Along with democracy, the country's independence and sovereignty could also be put at risk."
He was speaking at a discussion at the auditorium of the International University of Business Agriculture and Technology (IUBAT).
Rizvi alleged that the previous "fascist" government systematically dismantled democratic institutions, including the Election Commission, judiciary, and media, to remain in power indefinitely.
He said: "All democratic institutions were destroyed. The judiciary had no independence, the Election Commission became a tool for staging farcical elections and the media was tightly controlled. This is how fascist regimes operate everywhere."
He said opposition political parties were subjected to repression, citing the imprisonment of BNP Chairperson Khaleda Zia under harsh conditions as part of an effort to weaken the opposition.
Recalling the July uprising, Rizvi said the painful memories of those days remain vivid.
He said the July-August movement had restored some democratic space by ensuring conditions for a free and fair election and allowing people to criticize the government without fear of enforced disappearance or extrajudicial killing.
He said: "In a democratic society, people must be able to speak and criticize freely. That was absent before. The July movement restored that right. Its significance is therefore profound."
He called for further discussions between the government and political parties on the July Charter, saying any remaining issues should be resolved through dialogue.

item.title: HC issues rule to bar political figures from private school governing bodies
item.description:
The High Court on Monday issued a rule asking why individuals affiliated with political parties or holding political positions should not be declared ineligible for election, nomination or approval to the governing bodies and managing committees of private educational institutions by amending the existing regulations or framing new ones.
The court also sought an explanation as to why legal provisions should not be introduced to ensure the inclusion of locally respected education enthusiasts, retired teachers, persons of integrity and repute, freedom fighters, educationists, lawyers, physicians and other distinguished citizens in these committees. It further asked why judicial guidelines should not be issued in this regard.
The rule was issued by a High Court bench of Justice Ahmed Sohel and Justice Fatema Anwar after hearing a writ petition.
Advocates Md Nadir Khan and Md Sohel Rana represented the petitioner.
The writ was filed on June 7 in the public interest by Advocate Md Nadir Khan on behalf of a guardian, Rubel Mia.
The petition sought an amendment to Rule 7 of the Private Educational Institutions Governing Bodies and Managing Committees Regulations, 2024 , or the formulation of a new uniform regulation applicable to all education boards.
According to the petition, allowing individuals affiliated with political parties or holding party positions to serve on governing bodies and managing committees could compromise the neutrality, accountability and good governance of private educational institutions. It therefore urged the authorities to enact the necessary legal provisions to ensure the participation of respected, honest and education-minded members of society in these committees.

item.title: PM exchanges greetings with leaders of allied political parties
item.description:
Prime Minister Tarique Rahman on Monday exchanged greetings with leaders of the political parties that participated in the simultaneous anti-fascist movement at State Guest House Jamuna in Dhaka on Monday evening.
The prime minister arrived at the venue in the open courtyard of the State Guest House at 7:27 pm, where arrangements had been made for the leaders of the parties that took part in the joint movement.
Tarique Rahman went from table to table, exchanging greetings with the leaders of the allied leaders. He was accompanied by BNP Secretary General and LGRD and Cooperatives Mirza Fakhrul Islam Alamgir.
Later, the formal program began at 7:40 pm with the recitation of the Holy Quran.
Leaders of 41 political parties that had participated in the movement to oust the fascist government were invited to the event.
Members of the BNP Standing Committee and members of the liaison committee formed during the movement joined the event.
This is the first formal meeting between Prime Minister Tarique Rahman and the leaders of the parties allied in the anti-fascist movement since the BNP formed the government after securing a majority in the 13th parliamentary election held on February 12.
Among those invited were leaders of the Ganatantra Mancha, the 12-Party Alliance, the Like-Minded Nationalist Alliance, the Democratic Left Alliance, Islami Oikya Jote, and other Islamic political parties.

item.title: Three more children die with measles symptoms in Sylhet
item.description:
Three more children died of measles symptoms in Sylhet in the last 24 hours, raising the death toll from measles and measles-like symptoms in the division to 103.
The three children died while undergoing treatment at Sylhet MAG Osmani Medical College Hospital, Sylhet Divisional Office of Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) said on Monday.
Assistant Director of Sylhet Health Department Dr Noor-e-Alam said two of the deceased children, both under the age of two, were from Sunamganj, while the other was from Habiganj. Of the 103 victims who have died so far in Sylhet division, one was an adult and the rest were children.
Sunamganj has recorded the highest number of deaths in the division, with 47 children losing their lives so far. Besides, 35 deaths were reported in Sylhet, 12 in Moulvibazar and nine in Habiganj.
Meanwhile, 265 patients with measles symptoms are currently undergoing treatment at different hospitals across Sylhet Division.
According to the health department, a total of 534 laboratory-confirmed measles cases have been identified in the division since January 1, 2026. In the last 24 hours, 57 more patients with measles symptoms have been admitted to hospitals in Sylhet.
Authorities at Sylhet MAG Osmani Medical College Hospital said all-out efforts are being made to ensure proper treatment for patients. A total of 20 ICU beds have been set up at Osmani Medical College Hospital and 14 at Shaheed Shamsuddin Ahmed Hospital.

item.title: Journalist Saidur Rahman appointed press minister to New Delhi
item.description:
The government has appointed Md Saidur Rahman, a journalist at The Daily Ittefaq, as press minister at the Bangladesh High Commission in New Delhi, India.
A notification issued by the Ministry of Public Administration on Monday said he has been appointed for a 1-year term from the date of joining, subject to relinquishing all professional engagements, businesses, and positions with any government, semi-government, private institution, or other organization.
Saidur Rahman earned both his bachelor's and master's degrees from the Department of Mass Communication and Journalism at the University of Dhaka.
He began his journalism career as the University of Dhaka correspondent for Dainik Dinkal before joining The Daily Ittefaq.
Following the July Movement, he was promoted to the position of Politics and Election Editor at The Daily Ittefaq.
He previously served as general secretary of the Dhaka University Journalists' Association and as president of the Reporters Forum for Election and Democracy (RFED), an organization of journalists covering the Election Commission.

item.title: Jamuna erosion intensifies in Bogra as rising waters devour homes, cropland
item.description:
River erosion along the Jamuna River has intensified in Dhunat upazila of Bogra amid rising water levels caused by heavy rainfall and upstream inflows, leaving residents fearful of losing their homes, farmland and livelihoods.
Local residents say the erosion is moving closer to the flood control embankment, heightening concerns among thousands living along the riverbanks.
According to the Bogra Water Development Board (BWDB), the Jamuna's water level reached 15.57 metres on Saturday morning, rising nine centimetres over the previous 24 hours. Although the river remains 68 centimetres below the danger mark, water levels continue to increase.
Field visits to the affected areas found strong currents and whirlpools eroding riverbanks at an alarming rate. Farmland, trees and homesteads are being swallowed by the river as sections of soil collapse into the water.
The worst-hit area is a roughly 200-metre stretch between Shaharbari and Baniajan villages, where fresh land is disappearing into the river almost every day.
Farmers including Jewel Sheikh, Mizanur Rahman, Ziaul Haque and Asad Sheikh said they had already lost their homes and several bighas of farmland.
“Everything is disappearing before our eyes,” they said. “Within days, our land and houses have been washed away. If the erosion continues, Shaharbari village could one day disappear from the map.”
Residents alleged that despite severe erosion occurring almost every monsoon, no permanent river protection measures have been put in place. They warned that continued erosion could eventually threaten the flood control embankment.
BWDB Sub-Assistant Engineer Abdul Malek said increased pressure from upstream flows had triggered erosion in several riverside locations.
“Emergency protection work is underway using sand-filled geo-bags and tube dumping to curb the erosion,” he said.

item.title: ULAB, East West University hold river conservation field practicum
item.description:
The Department of General Education (GED) at the University of Liberal Arts Bangladesh (ULAB), in collaboration with Riverine People, organized a field practicum and sharing session titled "Conservation Learning through the Buriganga–Turag Confluence" on Saturday.
A total of 145 students from ULAB and East West University participated in the program.
The program brought together 105 students from four faculties of East West University and 40 students from ULAB. Accompanied by faculty members from both universities, the participants visited the confluence of the Buriganga and Turag rivers as part of an academic field exercise.
Sheikh Rokon, secretary general of Riverine People, briefed the participants on the historical significance of the Buriganga and Turag rivers. He discussed the major challenges facing the rivers, including encroachment and pollution, and highlighted initiatives undertaken by the government and civil society organizations. He also emphasized the role of students and young people in protecting and restoring the country's rivers.
Following the field visit, a sharing session was held at the ULAB cafeteria.
Professor Shahnaj Husne Jahan, head of the Department of General Education at ULAB, welcomed the participants and expressed appreciation for organizing the field practicum. She emphasized the importance of engaging students in river conservation efforts.
Mumita Tanjeela, associate professor in the Department of Sociology at East West University, appreciated the collaborative initiative and encouraged students to contribute to the conservation of natural resources.
A student representative from ULAB presented the department's activities, focusing on environmental protection and sustainable development initiatives. Srabonti Zaman, a ULAB student, also delivered a presentation on the university's environmental and sustainability initiatives, including river conservation.
F M Anwar Hossain, president of Riverine People, presented an overview of the organization's ongoing activities and research on the Buriganga River. He also outlined future plans and highlighted opportunities for collaboration with academic institutions and other stakeholders.
Among others, the sharing session was attended by Rasel Hussain, assistant professor; Helal Uddin, senior lecturer; and Ruma Parvin, senior lecturer, along with 2 student representatives from East West University. Rajat Kanti Roy, trustee and director (Riverine Archive) of Riverine People, also attended the event.
The program was facilitated by Ahmed Abidur Razzaque Khan, assistant professor in the Department of General Education at ULAB. Mohammed Mamun Or Rashid assisted in coordinating the field visit and managing the event.

item.title: Defense adviser: Cyber capability, local innovation must gain new momentum
item.description:
Bangladesh must strengthen its cyber capability and accelerate local innovation by transforming research into patents, usable technologies and locally developed products to enhance national security and reduce foreign dependence, Prime Minister's Defense Adviser Brigadier General (Retd) Dr AKM Shamsul Islam, PSC, G, said on Monday.
He made the remarks while visiting the Military Institute of Science and Technology (MIST) at Mirpur Cantonment in the capital.
During the visit, the defense adviser was briefed on the institution's modern education system, cybersecurity, research, patent, and technological innovation activities.
He first visited MIST's Cyber Range, where the head of the Computer Science and Engineering Department presented its capabilities, training activities, research opportunities and future plans.
The briefing highlighted the Cyber Range's role in addressing cyber threats, providing training in real-world simulated environments, developing skilled manpower and protecting the country's critical information and digital infrastructure.
The defence adviser said: "In today's world, a country's security is no longer confined to its geographical boundaries. Protecting information, communication systems and critical digital infrastructure is also an essential part of national security."
He emphasized the need to develop skilled cybersecurity professionals while strengthening coordination among educational institutions, government agencies and the domestic technology sector.
Dr AKM Shamsul Islam later met members of the MAVIROV team, who briefed him on their innovation activities, technological capabilities and future plans.
MIST's Research and Development Wing also presented the institution's work on patents and innovation, highlighting technologies developed by its teachers, researchers and students, research-based ideas and practical solutions with real-world applications.
Officials informed the defense adviser that MIST's MATEROV team won the MATEROV International Underwater Robot Competition 2026, held in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada, from June 23 to 27.
The competition featured 77 teams from 16 countries, with MIST securing the top position.
The defense adviser said: "Research achievements should not remain limited to reports or exhibitions. Effective research must be transformed into patents, usable technologies and locally developed products. This will enhance the country's technological capability, reduce foreign dependence and create new employment opportunities."
He stressed the importance of providing young innovators with research facilities, training, guidance and institutional support.
He also called for expanding research to address practical challenges in public welfare, disaster management, cybersecurity, health, agriculture, and industry.
MIST officials later briefed the defense adviser on the institution's ongoing education and research activities, technological capabilities and future development plans.
He praised the contributions of the institution's teachers, researchers, students and officials to advancing research and innovation.
The visit concluded with a photo session involving the defense adviser, members of the MAVIROV team and senior MIST officials.

item.title: Bangladesh moves toward Sustainable Building Labeling System
item.description:
Bangladesh on Monday took a step toward establishing its national Sustainable Building Labeling System as government officials, United Nations agencies, financial institutions, academics, professional bodies and private sector representatives attended a stakeholder consultation workshop in Dhaka.
The workshop was organized by the Housing and Building Research Institute (HBRI) under the Ministry of Housing and Public Works (MoHPW), in collaboration with the United Nations Human Settlements Program (UN-Habitat), the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) and the United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS), under the Transforming the Built Environment through Sustainable Materials in Bangladesh project funded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ).
The consultation focused on implementing HBRI-DESH (Design for Environment and Sustainable Habitats) and its digital platform, DESHboard, which will serve as Bangladesh's national labeling system for sustainable, affordable and climate-resilient buildings.
The inaugural session was attended by Most Ferdousi Begum, additional secretary and chairman of the National Housing Authority; Asifur Rahman Bhuiyan, chief architect of the Department of Architecture; and Dr Giasuddin Haider, director general of HBRI. A panel involving representatives from the Ministry of Housing and Public Works, Rajdhani Unnayan Kartripakkha (RAJUK), National Board of Revenue (NBR) and Dhaka Power Distribution Company Limited (DPDC) discussed policy, institutional coordination and financing for implementing the system.
According to the UNEP/GlobalABC Global Status Report 2025-2026, buildings and construction account for nearly 37% of global energy-related carbon dioxide emissions and around half of global material extraction. Bangladesh's urban population has roughly doubled in the past 2 decades and is projected to become majority urban by 2037, with housing demand expected to reach 10.5 million units by 2030, according to the International Finance Corporation (IFC).
Participants included representatives from government agencies, Bangladesh Bank, financial institutions, development partners, universities, professional bodies, real estate developers, the construction industry, environmental experts and civil society organizations.
Most Ferdousi Begum said: "HBRI-DESH is more than a technical framework—it is a nationally owned instrument that will help translate Bangladesh's commitment to sustainable urban development into practical action. Through strong collaboration among government, the private sector and development partners, we can establish a credible and transparent system that promotes sustainable, affordable and climate-resilient buildings across the country."
The consultation produced recommendations on governance, financing, capacity development and digital systems to inform HBRI-DESH's implementation guidelines and phased national rollout. The initiative also supports Bangladesh's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 11, 12 and 13.

item.title: Three more die of dengue, 372 hospitalized in 24hrs
item.description:
Three people died of dengue, and 372 new patients were admitted to hospitals across the country in the 24 hours to 8am Monday.
With the latest fatalities, the dengue death toll has risen to 37 this year, according to the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS).
The new deaths were reported in Dhaka South City Corporation, Khulna, and Barisal divisions, it said.
During the reporting period, 347 patients were discharged from hospitals after recovery.
Since January 1, a total of 10,857 dengue patients have been admitted to hospitals across the country. Of them, 9,786 have recovered and been discharged, while 1,034 patients are currently undergoing treatment.

item.title: EX-MP Jakob shown arrested in July uprising attempted murder case
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Monday showed former Bhola-4 lawmaker Abdullah Al Islam Jakob arrested in an attempted murder case filed over the shooting of a protester during the July uprising in Mirpur.
Dhaka Metropolitan Magistrate Ariful Islam passed the order after Sub Inspector Md Ashraful Doula and the investigating officer of Mirpur Model Police Station, sought to have Jakob shown arrested in the case.
Jakob was produced before the court wearing handcuffs, a bulletproof vest and a helmet, according to defence lawyer Md Khair Uddin. In the application, the investigating officer said Awami League activists allegedly attacked anti-discrimination student protesters and opened indiscriminate fire during the movement, leaving complainant Md Rifat Hossain with a bullet wound above his right knee.
The application further stated that Jakob, a former MP for Bhola-4 and an active Awami League member, needed to be shown arrested to ensure a fair investigation.
According to the case statement, Rifat joined the anti-discrimination student movement in front of Mirpur Girls' Ideal School and College in Mirpur Section-10 on the morning of August 4, 2024.
At around 2:00 pm, acting on the accused's instructions, 30 to 40 people, armed with firearms, locally made weapons and sticks, allegedly attacked the protesters and fired indiscriminately, leaving Rifat shot above his right knee.
The complaint also alleged that after he collapsed from the gunshot wound, the attackers kicked, punched and assaulted him with local weapons in an attempt to kill him. Fellow protesters rescued him and took him first to Islami Bank Hospital and later to Kurmitola General Hospital, where he underwent treatment for about a month. Doctors later removed the bullet from his leg through surgery.

Stakeholders call for sustainable financing to prevent child drowning
item.description:
Speakers at a stakeholder dialogue on Sunday called for sustainable financing and stronger multi-stakeholder collaboration to strengthen child drowning prevention efforts in Bangladesh.
They stressed that, alongside government funding, greater participation from the private sector, local government institutions, development partners, the media and other stakeholders is essential to ensure the long-term sustainability of drowning prevention initiatives.
The dialogue, titled "Prevention of Child Drowning and Sustainable Financing for Child Protection," was held at The Daily Star Centre in Dhaka. It was organised by Somashte Media Communication and Development Foundation, with support from the Global Health Advocacy Incubator (GHAI).
The event brought together government officials, representatives from the private sector, development partners, media professionals and public health experts.
Delivering the welcome address, Mir Masruruzzaman, executive director of Somashte, said an average of 40 children die from drowning every day in Bangladesh, yet the issue receives inadequate attention.
He said most child drowning incidents occur between 9am and 2pm, when caregivers are often occupied with work, leaving children without adequate supervision.
"A child can drown not only in large water bodies but also in shallow water. Ensuring safe supervision is therefore critical to preventing drowning," he said.
Rezaul Haque, Somashte's director of research and communications, presented an overview of drowning deaths in Bangladesh, the national strategy, the current financing framework and the potential role of stakeholders, including the private sector.
He said the national action plan aims to reduce drowning deaths by 50% by 2030. More than 18,000 people currently die from drowning each year. However, he said clearer funding sources, dedicated budget lines and expenditure-tracking mechanisms are needed to implement the plan effectively.
The presentation highlighted the Integrated Community-Based Childcare and SwimSafe (ICBC) project, which has provided childcare services, swimming lessons, CPR training, safe rescue education, water safety measures and awareness activities in 16 districts. The project's second phase is currently awaiting approval.
Professor Dr Kazi Maruful Islam of the Department of Development Studies at the University of Dhaka said drowning prevention should be viewed as a continuous social service rather than a project. He emphasised integrating such initiatives into existing local government structures.
Jamira Shabnam, deputy secretary of the Finance Division, called for coordinated initiatives prioritising high-risk areas. She said the central government, local government institutions and other stakeholders all have important roles to play in preventing drowning.
Reaz Ahmad, editor of Dhaka Tribune, said existing projects should be expanded rather than discontinued. He stressed the need for risk-based planning and data-driven management of drowning prevention efforts.
Azharul Haque Azad, managing director of Sadakalo, said raising public awareness about drowning is crucial. He added that collaboration between the media and business organisations could help build large-scale social initiatives.
Zahidul Islam Sajal, head of public communications at Nagad, said the social importance and measurable impact of drowning prevention must be clearly communicated to encourage greater private sector participation.
"Corporate organisations need to clearly understand both the importance of the issue and the opportunities for engagement," he said, adding that digital platforms also offer significant potential for public engagement and fundraising.
Sohrab Hasan, editor of Charcha.com, stressed the need to strengthen public awareness and community initiatives, particularly at the local level.
Media representatives, including Kajal Ghosh of Manab Zamin, Rabeya Baby of The Daily Ittefaq, Partha Shankar Saha of Prothom Alo, Zahid Rahman of Channel i and Saiduzzaman Rowshan of Agami Shomoy, discussed the role of the media in raising awareness and promoting policy attention to drowning prevention.
Sazia Afrin, communications manager at Synergos, said community acceptance of the ICBC project has been strong.
"The challenge now is ensuring these activities continue not only during the project period but also after it ends," she said.
Speakers concluded that while government funding should remain the primary source of financing, long-term sustainability will require complementary support from the private sector, corporate social responsibility initiatives, development partners and local stakeholders.
They also called for dedicated budget allocations across relevant ministries, stronger local government involvement, greater private sector participation and a transparent reporting mechanism to support effective implementation of drowning prevention initiatives.

Dhaka sees moderate air quality on Monday morning
item.description:
Dhaka, the capital of Bangladesh, witnessed moderate air quality on Monday morning, ranking 17th among cities with the worst air pollution worldwide.
At 9:45am, the capital recorded an Air Quality Index (AQI) score of 90, indicating moderate air quality, according to the air quality monitoring platform IQAir.
A moderate AQI indicates that air quality is generally acceptable. However, people who are unusually sensitive to air pollution may experience minor to moderate health effects if exposed for prolonged periods.
Kampala, Uganda, topped the list of the world's most polluted cities with an AQI score of 159, followed by Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, at 158.
Both cities recorded air quality classified as "unhealthy."
The AQI is calculated based on the concentration of major air pollutants, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), sulphur dioxide (SO₂) and ozone (O₃).
According to the AQI scale, a score between 51 and 100 is considered moderate, while 101–150 is deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, 151–200 is unhealthy, 201–300 is very unhealthy, and 301 or above is classified as hazardous, posing serious health risks to the entire population.
Dhaka has long struggled with air pollution, with air quality typically deteriorating during the dry winter months and improving during the monsoon season due to rainfall and stronger winds.
The World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes around seven million deaths globally each year, mainly due to stroke, heart disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer and acute respiratory infections.

item.title: Three new Sylhet wells to add 3 crore cubic feet of gas daily
item.description:
State-owned Sylhet Gas Fields Limited (SGFL) has taken an initiative to drill three new gas and oil wells as part of efforts to ease the country's gas shortage and reduce dependence on costly imports of liquefied natural gas (LNG).
The wells – Rashidpur-13, Dupitila-1 and Kailashtila-9 – are expected to add 3 crore cubic feet of gas per day to the national grid once drilling is completed, officials said.
At present, SGFL supplies around 14.20 crore cubic feet of gas daily to the national grid from its 17 functioning wells.
SGFL Managing Director Engr Md Faruque Hossain said: “SGFL is currently supplying 14.2 crore cubic feet of gas to the national grid every day. Once drilling of the three wells is completed, an additional 3 crore cubic feet of gas can be added to the grid."
Energy experts stressed the need to intensify domestic gas and oil exploration instead of relying heavily on LNG imports, which require substantial foreign currency expenditure each year.
They said Bangladesh has significant untapped hydrocarbon potential both onshore and offshore and called for expanding exploration activities through faster coordination with local and international companies.
Prof Dr Muhammad Farhad Howladar of the Department of Petroleum and Mining Engineering at Shahjalal University of Science and Technology said accelerating exploration activities is essential to ensuring the country's long-term energy security.
Established under Petrobangla, SGFL discovered Bangladesh's first natural gas field in 1955.
Apart from natural gas, the company's production currently meets around 30-35% of the country's petrol demand, 12-15% of octane demand, about 9% of diesel demand and nearly 2% of kerosene demand.

item.title: Dumuria farmers reap gains from growing crab farming
item.description:
Commercial crab farming has emerged as a profitable alternative to traditional rice and fish cultivation in Dumuria upazila of Khulna, transforming rural livelihoods and creating new opportunities for export earnings.
Favourable climatic conditions, easy access to saline water and strong international demand have encouraged many unemployed youths and small farmers to engage in crab fattening, making it one of the fastest-growing aquaculture sectors in the region.
Farmers said crab farming requires relatively low investment, faces fewer disease risks than conventional fish farming and produces market-ready crabs within three to four months. Both soft-shell and hard-shell crab fattening methods are widely practised, offering attractive returns.
Of the 15 crab species found in Bangladesh, 11 are saltwater species and four are freshwater species. The red crab is considered the most commercially important and is the largest among them.
Visits to different villages in Dumuria found that crab farming has become increasingly popular due to its profitability and growing market demand.
Local farmers said they once relied mainly on paddy cultivation. Before Cyclone Aila, most land in the area was used for crop production. However, changing environmental conditions and the expansion of saline water have encouraged many farmers to switch to crab farming.
More than 5,000 farmers, including many women, have become self-reliant through crab farming in the upazila. Although Dumuria has long been known for agriculture and fisheries, it is now emerging as a major hub for crab cultivation.
According to the Department of Fisheries, nearly 28,000 farmers across Khulna Division, including around 9,000 women, have improved their livelihoods through training and marketing support related to crab farming.
Dumuria Senior Fisheries Officer Sohel Md Zillur Rahman Rigan said the area's soil and water are highly suitable for crab cultivation.
"The Department of Fisheries is providing technical support, guidance on quality seed selection and proper farm management. As crab farming involves lower risks and higher profits, more entrepreneurs are entering the sector, contributing significantly to the rural economy," he said.
Khulna District Fisheries Officer Md Badruzzaman said crabs produced in the region enjoy strong demand in international markets, including China, Singapore, Malaysia, Hong Kong, Thailand, South Korea, Taiwan, Australia, Japan and the United Arab Emirates.
He said the government is working to strengthen market linkages, ensure fair prices for farmers, reduce dependence on middlemen and promote environmentally sustainable crab farming.
Ashutosh Ghosh, a farmer from Tipna village, said his family turned to crab farming because it is more profitable than fish or paddy cultivation.
Monali Saha, another farmer, said crab farming has become her family's primary source of income.
"My husband and I have been farming crabs on one acre of land for the past five years. However, middlemen still earn more than we do because we lack direct access to markets," she said.
Farmers Salman Sheikh, Swapan Mondal and Rishikesh Das said many households that once struggled with debt have achieved financial stability through crab farming, enabling them to build permanent homes and purchase land.
They called for easier access to credit and expanded hatchery facilities for artificial seed production, saying such support could help crab farming become a major export-earning industry alongside Bangladesh's shrimp sector.

item.title: Rizvi: Court, EC to decide AL leaders' eligibility for local polls
item.description:
BNP Senior Joint Secretary General and Political Adviser to the Prime Minister Advocate Ruhul Kabir Rizvi on Sunday said the eligibility of leaders and activists of Awami League to contest the upcoming local government elections would be determined by the court and the Election Commission (EC) in accordance with the law.
He made the remarks while speaking to reporters after a BNP delegation, led by him, submitted the party's audited income and expenditure statement for the January–December 2025 period to the Election Commission at the Election Bhaban in Agargaon.
Asked whether leaders and activists of the Awami League would be allowed to contest the upcoming local elections, Rizvi said the polls would not be held on party symbols.
"Whether someone can contest or not is a matter for the courts and the Election Commission. They will decide in accordance with the prevailing laws," he said.
Rizvi said it was the Election Commission's responsibility, not that of political parties, to determine who is eligible to contest under the existing legal framework and any new laws that may be enacted.
"We leave the matter to the Election Commission," he added.
Earlier, Election Commissioner Abdur Rahmanel Mashud told Dhaka Tribune that a candidate's political affiliation would not determine eligibility for the local government elections. Instead, eligibility would be assessed based on the legal criteria for qualification and disqualification, meaning anyone meeting those requirements would be allowed to contest.
The Election Commission plans to hold elections to several tiers of local government bodies in October, with the election schedule likely to be announced by the end of August or in September. Union Parishad and municipality elections are expected to be held first.
Responding to a question on whether the BNP would endorse candidates in the local government polls, Rizvi said that since the elections would not be conducted on party symbols, the party's highest policymaking body would decide the issue.
"No decision has been taken yet," he said.

item.title: Asaduzzaman: Govt ensuring taxpayers' money to spend for public welfare
item.description:
Law, Justice and Parliamentary Affairs Minister Md Asaduzzaman has said the taxpayers' money is spent solely for the welfare of the people as it is one of the government's key commitments, and the government is fulfilling that commitment through the proper and transparent use of public funds.
"The government has been working relentlessly to ensure the proper and transparent use of public funds so that the benefits of development reach people in every corner of the country," the minister said.
He made the remarks while addressing a function as the chief guest at the Magura District Auditorium on Sunday. At the program, organized by the Magura Zila Parishad, bicycles were distributed among students, sewing machines among rural women and saplings among beneficiaries, an official release said on Sunday.
“In the past, a significant portion of the funds allocated for public welfare remained unspent in practice due to corruption and irregularities, existing only on paper. Present government has broken away from that culture by ensuring accountability. The government is pursuing a zero-tolerance policy against corruption. Investigations are underway into funds allegedly misappropriated in the name of development projects in the past, while legal action is being taken against those responsible,” the law minister added.
Md Asaduzzaman further said the government has been working consistently to fulfil its election pledges. It has already taken initiatives to implement the Family Card, Agriculture Card and Imam Card programs.
He said all eligible families across the country would gradually be brought under the Family Card program, through which financial assistance would be provided to women. At the same time, various support programs are also being implemented for farmers.
The function was chaired by Magura Zila Parishad Administrator Ali Ahmed. Magura Sadar lawmaker Md Monowar Hosen, Deputy Commissioner Motakabbir Ahmed and others also addressed the program.
A total of 182 beneficiaries received assistance under the program.
Earlier, the law minister addressed a program organized by the Magura District Bar Association as the chief guest.

item.title: Envoy: Beijing to work with Dhaka to implement key consensus reached by two leaders
item.description:
Chinese Ambassador to Bangladesh Yao Wen has said his country is willing to work with Bangladesh to implement the important consensus reached by the leaders of the two countries.
He also said China is willing to deepen exchanges on governance experiences, strengthen practical cooperation with Bangladesh across various fields, including the military sector, and better benefit both countries and their peoples.
Ambassador Yao attended and addressed a seminar on high-level visits on Saturday.
The event was attended by over a hundred participants, including LGRD and Cooperatives Minister Mirza Fakhrul Islam Alamgir, Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon, and State Minister for Civil Aviation and Tourism M Rashiduzzaman Millat.
Ambassador Yao said Prime Minister Tarique Rahman's recent visit to China has yielded fruitful results, and the two countries agreed to elevate the comprehensive strategic cooperative partnership between Bangladesh and China to build a Bangladesh-China community with a shared future in the new era.
He said China has always attached great importance to developing bilateral relations with Bangladesh and will always be a trustworthy good friend, good neighbor, and good partner of Bangladesh.
The speakers contributed their views, agreeing that bilateral relations are at their best historical juncture, and expressing anticipation for joint efforts between the two countries to advance the building of a Bangladesh-China community with a shared future in the new era, said the Embassy on Sunday.

item.title: BMD: Rainfall likely to continue over next 5 days
item.description:
The Bangladesh Meteorological Department (BMD) has predicted that rainfall is likely to continue across the country over the next five days, commencing at 9am on Sunday, as the monsoon is active over Bangladesh.
“Light to moderate rain or thunder showers accompanied by temporary gusty wind is likely to occur at most places over the Rangpur, Mymensingh, and Sylhet divisions and at many places over the Rajshahi, Dhaka, Khulna, Barisal, and Chittagong divisions, with moderately heavy to heavy falls at places over the country,” said a regular bulletin of the Met Office.
Day and night temperatures may remain nearly unchanged over the country.
The axis of monsoon troughs runs through Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and West Bengal to Assam across the northern part of Bangladesh.
One of its associated troughs extends up to the Northwest Bay. Monsoon is active over the northern part and fairly active elsewhere over the country, and monsoon is moderate over North Bay.

item.title: Yunus joins Nobel Laureates in AI, nuclear declaration
item.description:
A joint declaration has been signed at the Global Nobel Laureates Assembly on Artificial Intelligence and Nuclear War, warning that artificial intelligence and nuclear weapons together pose an unprecedented danger to humanity's future.
Professor Muhammad Yunus joined fellow Nobel Laureates, including Romano Prodi, Jody Williams, Maria Ressa, Denis Mukwege, and Juan Manuel Santos, at the event, said a press release.
The Assembly opened at Borgo Laudato Si', Castel Gandolfo, with a ceremony featuring an opening address from Professor Yunus, alongside welcome remarks from Cardinal Fabio Baggio, C.S., President of Borgo Laudato Si', an opening address from Cardinal Ángel Fernández Artime, S.D.B., Pro-Prefect of the Dicastery for the Institutes of Consecrated Life and Societies of Apostolic Life, and introductory remarks from Cardinal Silvano Maria Tomasi, President Emeritus of the Domus Communis Foundation and President of the Global Nobel Laureates Assembly.
On the second day of the assembly, Professor Yunus took part in a high-level session on artificial intelligence, democracy, and the economic revolution: toward a new world order, where he delivered an address.
He also held a series of bilateral meetings on the sidelines of the assembly, including with Cardinal Mauro Gambetti, archpriest of Saint Peter's Basilica and president of the Fabric of Saint Peter, and Dr Qu Dongyu, director-general of the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO).
The three-day assembly concluded on Thursday, with the signing of the declaration at Rome's Capitoline Hill. Titled "Humanity at the Threshold," the declaration draws a direct parallel between the dawn of the nuclear age and the current AI era, arguing that the world risks repeating the mistakes of the past by entering an AI arms race alongside a worsening nuclear one.
The laureates warn that AI is eroding human judgment in nuclear command systems, fueling cyber threats to critical infrastructure, and enabling information warfare that undermines the shared trust nations need for peace.
Invoking Pope Leo XIV's call for an "unarmed and disarming peace," the signatories reject the idea that security can be built on fear or mutual destruction. They set out six principles.
1. Disarming the next arms race – urging restraint and cooperative governance to prevent AI and nuclear competition from shaping the coming century.
2. Responsible development – calling for transparency and accountability from AI developers and an outright ban on unsupervised, fully automated recursive self-improvement in AI systems.
3. Responsible use – demanding an international treaty to keep AI out of nuclear launch decisions and to protect nuclear infrastructure from cyber interference.
4. Responsible governance – supporting the UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and new international institutions to oversee AI development.
5. Responsible leadership – emphasizing the role of young people and education in confronting these threats.
6. Nuclear disarmament—renewing calls for time-bound, verifiable elimination of nuclear weapons and reinforcing existing treaties, including the Non-Proliferation Treaty and the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons.
The declaration closes by invoking Bertrand Russell and Albert Einstein's appeal to "remember your humanity, and forget the rest," framing the moment as one on which the survival of future generations depends.
Professor Daniel Holz, Chair of the Science and Security Board of the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists; Cardinal Peter Turkson, Chancellor of the Pontifical Academies of Sciences; Archbishop Renzo Pegoraro, President of the Pontifical Academy for Life; a wider gathering of Nobel Laureates; and actress Sharon Stone also joined.

SAAB elects new executive committee at Russian House in Dhaka
item.description:
The Soviet and Russian Alumni Association of Bangladesh (SAAB) on Friday elected its 14th Executive Committee at the Russian House in Dhaka, with nearly 400 members from across the country casting their votes.
Established on August 7, 1982, SAAB is one of Bangladesh's leading alumni organizations, representing more than 6,000 graduates of universities and higher education institutions in the former Soviet Union and the Russian Federation.
Its members have contributed significantly to Bangladesh's development in diverse sectors, including education, science, engineering, medicine, research, public administration, business and culture.
The election resulted in a new 22-member Executive Committee, which will lead the organization for the next term. The committee is expected to strengthen SAAB's activities while promoting educational, scientific, cultural and humanitarian cooperation between Bangladesh and Russia.
Speaking at the event, Alexandra Khlevnaya, director of the Russian House in Dhaka, congratulated the newly elected committee and wished its members a successful tenure.
She expressed confidence that SAAB would continue to play a vital role in fostering friendship, mutual understanding, and academic and cultural exchanges between Bangladesh and Russia.
The Russian House also reaffirmed its support for SAAB and its alumni network, highlighting their contribution to strengthening bilateral ties.
It further underscored Russia's commitment to expanding higher education opportunities and government scholarship programmes for Bangladeshi students, expressing hope that future graduates of Russian universities would continue contributing to Bangladesh's socio-economic development while serving as a bridge between the two countries.
The election reflected the enduring partnership between Bangladesh and Russia in higher education, research and people-to-people exchanges, underscoring the growing role of alumni networks in deepening bilateral cooperation.

item.title: Kuwait says Iran hit second power, water plant
IAILC Prestige Awards 2026 ceremony set for July 25
item.description:
The UHF Global Consortium has announced that organizations and individuals selected for the IAILC Prestige Awards 2026 have been notified following an international evaluation process.
According to the consortium, Official Confirmation Letters have been sent to all selected awardees via email and courier after a comprehensive evaluation that included research, due diligence, governance review, and a transparent selection process.
The consortium said the IAILC Prestige Awards 2026 ceremony will be held on July 25, 2026, at the Pan Pacific Sonargaon Hotel in Dhaka. The event will take place alongside the Global Conference on AI Leadership & Flagship Prestige Awards 2026, bringing together leaders, institutions, and professionals from Bangladesh and abroad.
To receive their awards and participate in both events, all awardees are required to complete the official free online registration.
Awardees must access the official registration portal and submit the details of 1 or more official representatives, including name, designation, mobile number, email address, and other required contact information.
The consortium said there is no registration fee, administrative fee, processing fee, or sponsorship fee, and that the registration process is free of charge.
Upon successful registration, awardees will receive an Official Registration ID, VIP Delegate Pass, Event Pass, Awards Ceremony Entry Authorization, and Conference Accreditation.
The consortium said completing registration within the specified timeframe will allow organizers to prepare each awardee's official medal, recognition crest, recognition certificate, Continuing Professional Development (CPD) dual certification, delegate ID, and other event materials on time.
The UHF Global Consortium encouraged all awardees to complete their registration promptly to participate in the international event.

Measles outbreak: Death toll reaches 784 as four die in 24 hours
item.description:
Four children died with symptoms consistent with measles in the 24 hours until 8:00am on Saturday, bringing Bangladesh’s combined tally of confirmed and suspected measles-related deaths to 784.
The Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) has classified the latest deaths as suspected measles fatalities.
With the latest update, the number of suspected measles deaths has risen to 689, while the number of laboratory-confirmed measles deaths remained unchanged at 95.
A total of 699 new suspected measles cases were reported during the period, bringing the cumulative number of suspected cases nationwide to 116,710.
Meanwhile, 74 new confirmed measles cases were recorded, bringing the total number of laboratory-confirmed infections to 14,318. Since March 15, a total of 99,249 patients with suspected measles have been hospitalised across the country, of whom 95,529 have recovered, according to DGHS data.

item.title: Court fixes Sept 1 for probe report in infant murder case
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Friday ordered police to submit an investigation report by September 1 in a case filed against a man accused of killing his seven-month-old daughter by throwing her to the ground following a domestic dispute in the capital's Turag area.
The first information report (FIR) was placed before the court on Friday. Dhaka Metropolitan Magistrate Hasibuzzaman accepted the case and directed the investigating officer to submit the probe report by the scheduled date.
SI Helal Uddin of the prosecution division confirmed the matter.
The victim's maternal grandmother, Sharmin Akter, filed the murder case with Turag Police Station on Friday, accusing the child's father, Kabir Islam.
Investigating officer SI Mehedi Hasan Faisal said Kabir has been arrested.
According to the case statement, Kabir Islam married Lima Akter two years ago and the couple had a daughter Rafa Moni Kabita. A rickshaw puller by profession, Kabir had allegedly been addicted to drugs for a long time and frequently assaulted and verbally abused his wife without provocation.
On Thursday, Lima visited her ailing father with the infant. At around 7:00 pm, an argument broke out between the couple over a family matter at their residence in Harun Molla's slum in Balur Math, Uttara.
The complaint alleges that Kabir became enraged, snatched the baby from her grandmother's arms, lifted her above his head and slammed her onto the road.
The critically injured infant was rushed to Dhaka Medical College Hospital, where she was pronounced dead at around 2:30 pm while undergoing treatment.

item.title: Info minister: Economy is the foundation of Bangladesh's foreign policy
item.description:
Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon on Saturday said Bangladesh's foreign policy is guided by economic priorities rather than geopolitical considerations, with trade, investment, remittances and overseas employment shaping the country's diplomatic engagements.
Addressing a seminar titled "Long Live Bangladesh-China Friendship", organized by the Bangladesh-China Friendship Association (BCFA) at a hotel in the capital, he said China has the potential to become one of Bangladesh's most strategic and dependable partners in its economic advancement.
Local Government, Rural Development and Cooperatives Minister Mirza Fakhrul Islam Alamgir addressed the event as the chief guest.
Chinese Ambassador Yao Wen was present as the guest of honor, while Bangladesh-China Friendship Association President and Adviser to the BNP Chairman Advocate Nazmul Haque Nannu chaired the program.
State Minister for Civil Aviation and Tourism M Rashiduzzaman Millat also addressed the seminar as a special guest. Lawmakers Sanjida Islam Tulee and Md Shamsur Rahman Simul Biswas, among others, attended the event.
Referring to the growing economic partnership between Bangladesh and China, Swapon said China is Bangladesh's second-largest source of foreign direct investment (FDI), while bilateral trade has reached nearly US$20 billion.
Noting that Bangladesh's exports to China remain below US$1 billion, he said the Chinese government is keen to support Bangladesh in expanding its export capacity.
The minister said China could emerge as Bangladesh's most important development partner in the coming years under the vision of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman.
Emphasizing the importance of regional connectivity for economic growth, Swapon said no country can achieve rapid development without improving transport links and regional integration.
He said a significant strategic initiative had recently been completed through the efforts of Chinese Ambassador Yao Wen, leading to an understanding between Bangladesh and China to establish road connectivity.
Swapon said that overcoming several long-standing complexities, the process of establishing road connectivity among China, Myanmar and Bangladesh is set to begin.
In the interest of regional development, any other country, including India, would be welcomed to join the initiative which would be regarded as a major strategic victory for the region's economy, he added.
Turning to the Rohingya crisis, Swapon said Bangladesh expects China to play a stronger and more effective role in facilitating a lasting solution through dialogue between Bangladesh and Myanmar.
"The Chinese government has attached importance to the mutual interests of Bangladesh and Myanmar and we hope China will make meaningful contributions to resolving the Rohingya issue," he said.
Clarifying the foundation of Bangladesh's diplomatic outlook, the minister said, Bangladesh believes in a pragmatic policy. Geopolitics is not our primary objective; our priority is the economy.
We import from China and India, export to the United States and Europe and receive remittances from the Middle East and Malaysia. Our foreign policy reflects these economic realities and China attaches great importance to this position," he said.
Swapon also praised Chinese Ambassador Yao Wen, describing him as one of the key figures in strengthening Bangladesh-China friendship and expanding political and economic cooperation between the two countries.

item.title: Fakhrul: BNP wants to further strengthen geopolitical, business ties with China
item.description:
BNP Secretary General and LGRD and Cooperatives Minister Mirza Fakhrul Islam Alamgir on Saturday said the ruling BNP wants to further strengthen its geopolitical, economic, and commercial relations with China.
He said the existing relationship between the two countries had been further reinforced through the recent visit of Bangladesh's Prime Minister Tarique Rahman to China.
Mirza Fakhrul was addressing a discussion titled "Long Live Bangladesh-China Friendship" at a hotel in the city organized by the Bangladesh-China Friendship Association following the premier visit to China. Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon and State Minister for Civil Aviation and Tourism M Rashiduzzaman Millat were present while Chinese Ambassador to Bangladesh Yao Wen attended the event as the guest of honor.
President of the Bangladesh-China Friendship Association and BNP chairman's adviser Advocate Nazmul Haque Nannu chaired it.
Fakhrul said Tarique Rahman's visit to China has further energized bilateral relations, while Bangladeshi youths are going to China on different occasions and building relationships there.
The BNP has maintained a long-standing relationship with the Communist Party of China, said Fakhrul.
"Even during the fascist Awami League regime, we maintained communication with the Communist Party of China," he said.
"The BNP wants to maintain relations with China based on geopolitical interests. We also want to maintain economic and political relations with India and the United States," Fakhrul said.
He said relations between China and Bangladesh are now at a very high level. Bangladesh seeks China's technological assistance, he said, adding, "We also need China's support to provide technical and vocational education for Bangladeshi youth."
Fakhrul noted that Bangladesh would benefit from greater Chinese technological support in road and infrastructure construction, solar energy, and green energy development.
He also said, "We are optimistic. The BNP shares ideological similarities with China. We have business relations with China. We will import from China and export to China, and China will likewise import from and export to Bangladesh. Bangladesh looks to China's leadership as a force against hegemony."
Addressing the meeting, Chinese ambassador Yao Wen said China is interested in introducing its high-speed rail technology in Bangladesh in the future.
He said it would be possible to connect Chittagong, Dhaka, and Sylhet through such a high-speed rail network.
The ambassador also assured Bangladesh of China's fullest cooperation in the repatriation of the Rohingyas.
He said Tarique Rahman's visit had marked the beginning of a new chapter in bilateral relations.

item.title: PM for using environment-friendly technologies for brick production
item.description:
Prime Minister Tarique Rahman on Sunday put emphasis on producing bricks using environmentally friendly alternative technologies to prevent air pollution.
He laid the importance while chairing a meeting on environment pollution at his Tejgaon office in the city, said Prime Minister's Assistant Press Secretary Gazi Shahriar Pamy.
He said the meeting held detailed discussions on a range of measures to prevent air and sound pollution. In particular, it decided to take swift and effective actions to curb air pollution caused by brick kilns.
Besides, the meeting discussed ways to control the excessive use of vehicle horns in the capital city Dhaka. In this regard, the prime minister instructed the law enforcement agencies to play a more stringent role in enforcing the law.
It also discussed how artificial intelligence (AI) technology, similar to AI cameras used in traffic management, could be utilized to monitor and control unnecessary horn use.
The prime minister emphasized the need for effective initiatives to quickly remove unfit, expired and environment polluting buses, trucks and other vehicles from the streets of the capital.
He also directed the authorities to introduce automatic traffic light systems at at least 50 more locations across Dhaka as quickly as possible.
Environment, Forest and Climate Change Minister Abdul Awal Mintoo, State Minister Shaikh Faridul Islam, State Minister for Water Resources Forhad Hossain Azad, Prime Minister's Special Assistant on Environment, Forest and Climate Change Affairs Dr Md Saimum Parvez, Dhaka South City Corporation Administrator Md Abdus Salam, Dhaka North City Corporation Administrator Md Shafiqul Islam Khan, Dhaka Metropolitan Police Commissioner Mosleh Uddin Ahmed, Additional Police Commissioner (Traffic) Md Anisur Rahman and senior officials from various ministries and agencies were present at the meeting.

Trump questions England's World Cup tactics
item.description:
To the long list of critics of England's tactics in the World Cup semi-final they lost to Argentina can now be added Donald Trump.
The US president on Friday took a gentle swipe at coach Thomas Tuchel's decision to adopt defensive tactics after England took the lead against Argentina on Wednesday.
Speaking at a reception in New York alongside FIFA president Gianni Infantino, Trump mentioned England captain Harry Kane, with whom he shared a round of golf in Florida 18 months ago.
"You have a great player in England, who I played golf with, Harry," Trump said.
"They perhaps made a mistake when they made him a defensive player. What do I know about soccer?" Trump added.
"They took the lead and they took their best player and they put him on defence.
"We got to be a little offensive, right? What do I know about coaching?"
England coach Thomas Tuchel was heavily criticised for withdrawing two forwards and putting on defenders in the last half hour of the game against Argentina, meaning England had to sit back although Kane stayed on the field.
Lionel Messi exploited the space and provided the assists that led to the two Argentine goals that sent the South Americans into the final.
Tuchel repeated on Friday that he stood by his tactical decisions.
"No I don't regret my decision. I thought we had become too passive," Tuchel said. "I was trying to help the team."
